hypotheses.text: Increased screen time during early childhood is associated with elevated ADHD symptom scores at age 8, after controlling for socioeconomic status and parental education.
hypotheses.rationale: Multiple longitudinal cohort studies have examined this association but findings remain inconsistent due to differences in measurement and control variables.

hypotheses.text: The association between screen time and ADHD symptoms is partially mediated by reduced sleep duration and quality.
hypotheses.rationale: Sleep disruption is a known consequence of excessive screen exposure and is independently linked to attention problems, yet formal mediation analyses are scarce.

raw_papers.title: Association of screen time with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptoms in children: A longitudinal cohort study
raw_papers.abstract: A prospective cohort study of 2,441 children followed from ages 3 to 5. Higher screen time at age 3 was associated with greater ADHD symptom scores at age 5 (beta = 0.13, 95% CI: 0.07 to 0.19), after adjusting for confounders.

raw_papers.title: Screen time and sleep problems in school-aged children: A systematic review and dose-response meta-analysis
raw_papers.abstract: A meta-analysis of 32 studies found a significant dose-response relationship between daily screen time and sleep problems (OR = 1.32 per additional hour), with stronger effects for interactive screen use.

raw_papers.title: Sleep duration mediates the relationship between screen time and attention problems in early adolescence
raw_papers.abstract: Cross-sectional analysis of 1,876 adolescents aged 11–14. Screen time was associated with attention problems (beta = 0.09, p < 0.01), and this relationship was partially mediated by sleep duration (indirect effect = 0.03, p < 0.05).
